summary: Nadia provides a practical, demystified guide to achieving the jhanas — eight progressive altered mental states culminating in cessation (loss of consciousness) — which she learned in roughly 20 hours across two retreats despite minimal prior meditation experience. She frames jhanas as a body-encoded technology akin to software, arguing that the Buddhist theoretical scaffolding surrounding them unnecessarily gatekeeps what is essentially a learnable skill. She details her specific techniques for each stage, from inducing euphoria through sustained attention on joy, to navigating the dissociative and reality-dissolving higher states. Ultimately, she argues that the jhanas themselves are not the point — they are an algorithm for arriving at fundamental insights about consciousness, after which the practice feels genuinely complete.
key_points: Jhanas are a learnable technology encoded in our bodies, not an esoteric Buddhist practice requiring years of meditation experience — Nadia progressed through all nine states in about 20 hours
The key mechanism is sustained, concentrated attention acting as an amplifier on positive emotions, essentially inducing 'the opposite of a panic attack'

The jhanas progress from euphoria (J1) through contentment and peace (J2-J4), then into dissociative states dissolving space, consciousness, and perception (J5-J8), culminating in cessation of consciousness (J9)
Jhanas represent a form of 'natural psychedelics' that could offer therapeutic benefits without chemical interventions or regulatory barriers

The real value lies not in the altered states themselves but in the insights gained — particularly after cessation, which prompted philosophical reflections that left the practice feeling genuinely complete
money_quotes: Jhanas are an algorithm in the oldest sense of the word: a set of instructions that, if executed correctly, solve for a problem that you may not have even realized you've been trying to unravel.
They are an Easter egg hiding in the game of life.

If you had recently figured out how to code, and realized it was really quite simple and teachable to others – then looked around, and all you saw were computer scientists warning off would-be developers from making software – wouldn't that make you want to open up a text editor and type out your own version of things?
Jhanas are the closest thing to magic that I've experienced in my adult life.
I don't think the jhanas made me happy, but their biggest impact was enabling me to realize how happy I already was.
The jhanas are useful for tinkering with the mind, but after cessation, I realized that neither body nor mind is really all that important.
How often does mastery of an activity end with true fulfillment, rather than boredom, distraction, or disinterest?
One day, we might look back on psychedelics as an early, coarse attempt to do this sort of thing that came with all sorts of weird side effects, like dentists using cocaine in the late 1800s.
key_insight: Jhanas are a learnable, body-encoded technology for systematically intensifying positive mental states through concentrated attention, but their ultimate purpose is not the altered states themselves — it is the fundamental insights about consciousness that emerge from completing the full progression through cessation.
